settings.title: Ten Years, Forty Tonnes of Compost
settings.excerpt: A decade in, we did the sums on what we have planted, and the number that stuck with us was the soil.
settings.body:
This month marks ten years since the first Greenfield job, a tired front garden three streets from where the yard stands now. We started with one van, a borrowed rotavator, and more enthusiasm than sense.

We pulled the records for fun. In ten years we have planted just over two thousand trees and shrubs and spread roughly forty tonnes of compost we make from our own green waste. The compost number is the one that stuck with us, because none of it left as rubbish. It went back into the ground.

Ten years in numbers

  • Over two thousand trees and shrubs planted
  • Roughly forty tonnes of our own compost spread
  • One yard, four staff, and a great many cups of tea

Making our own compost started as a way to cut tip runs and ended up changing how we work. Hedge trimmings, leaf fall, and turf all go through the heaps and come back as soil improver, so a garden we tidy in autumn often feeds itself the following spring.

To mark the decade, we are donating a full planting day to the community garden by the canal, which has kept volunteers busy for years on a shoestring. New beds, fresh soil, and a small orchard going in at the back. The apple and plum trees should be cropping in a few years, long after the work itself is forgotten.

Thank you to every client who let us dig up their garden and trusted us to put it back better. Here is to the next ten, and to the trees from year one that are now taller than the people who watched us plant them.
